subheading: Run Kuaishou's Kling video model in ComfyUI: text-to-video, image-to-video, and first-last-frame, with character reference, on Comfy Cloud.
extendedDescription:
The practical starting point for Kling comfyui workflows is that Kling is a cloud video model from Kuaishou, so these workflows call it through ComfyUI's API nodes instead of loading weights. There is no local download; what you get is access to a well-regarded video generator wired into editable graphs, where the prompt, the conditioning image, and the output all pass through nodes you can adjust and chain into the rest of a pipeline.

The workflows cover the common video jobs. Text-to-video turns a written prompt into a clip, image-to-video animates a still you upload, and first-last-frame (FLF2V) interpolates between two frames you set so the motion starts and ends where you want. Kling also supports character reference, where you supply an image of a subject and the model carries that likeness into the generated motion, which is useful for keeping a person or character recognizable across clips.

Kling has moved through several versions, up through the current Kling 3.0 flagship that supersedes the earlier O-series and Omni builds, and the workflows target the latest release so you get its motion quality and prompt adherence without tracking version names yourself. Kling 3.0 pushes resolution to native 4K and can generate synchronized audio in the same pass on supported tasks. Because the graph is open, you keep control of the prompt, the conditioning frames, the duration, and the aspect ratio, even though the model itself is hosted and fixed.

To run one, open a Kling workflow on Comfy Cloud with nothing to install, since there is no local option. Write your prompt or upload your image or frames, set the duration and aspect ratio, and generate. You can re-run with a tighter prompt or a different reference until the motion and timing are right, and the clip drops straight into your wider ComfyUI workflow.

modelSpec.summary: Kling is Kuaishou's cloud video-generation model, accessed through ComfyUI API nodes. It handles text-to-video, image-to-video, and first-last-frame, with character-reference support, up through the current Kling 3.0 flagship with native 4K output.
highlights: Cloud model from Kuaishou; no public weights, run through ComfyUI API nodes
Character reference carries a supplied subject likeness into the motion
Current Kling 3.0 flagship adds native 4K output and synchronized audio on supported tasks
Control over prompt, conditioning frames, duration, and aspect ratio
Runs on Comfy Cloud with no install and no GPU needed on your end

howToUse: Pick a Kling workflow: text-to-video, image-to-video, or first-last-frame.
Open it on Comfy Cloud, which runs the ComfyUI API nodes that call Kling.
Write your prompt, or upload the still image or the first and last frames.
Add a character-reference image if the workflow supports it, then set the duration.
Choose the aspect ratio, generate the clip, and review the motion and timing.
Re-run with a tighter prompt or different reference, then download the result.

faqItems.question: What are Kling ComfyUI workflows?
faqItems.answer: They are ready-made ComfyUI graphs that run Kling, Kuaishou's cloud video model, through ComfyUI's API nodes. Each wires up a task like kling text to video or kling image to video, so you give a prompt or an image and get a clip without assembling the graph yourself.

faqItems.question: Can I run Kling locally on my own GPU?
faqItems.answer: No. Kling has no public weights to download, so it runs as a cloud model through ComfyUI's API nodes rather than on local hardware. Comfy Cloud handles the compute, so you do not need a powerful GPU on your end.

faqItems.question: What can Kling generate?
faqItems.answer: Kling handles text-to-video, image-to-video, and first-last-frame interpolation between two frames you set. It also supports character reference, where a supplied image of a subject is carried into the generated motion.

faqItems.question: Does Kling keep a character consistent across clips?
faqItems.answer: Yes, through its character-reference support. You supply an image of the subject, and the model carries that likeness into the motion, which helps keep a person or character recognizable from one clip to the next.

faqItems.question: What Kling version do these workflows use?
faqItems.answer: The workflows target the current Kling releases, up through the 3.0 and O-series models. That means you get the latest motion quality and prompt adherence without having to track version names yourself.

faqItems.question: What does first-last-frame mean in a Kling workflow?
faqItems.answer: First-last-frame, or FLF2V, lets you set the starting frame and the ending frame, and Kling generates the motion in between. It gives you more control over where a clip begins and ends than a single prompt or image alone.

faqItems.question: Do I need to install anything to use these workflows?
faqItems.answer: No local install is required. Because Kling is a hosted model with no public weights, you open the workflow on Comfy Cloud in the browser and the API nodes call the model for you. There is no GPU setup and no model download on your end.

faqItems.question: How much does it cost to generate Kling clips this way?
faqItems.answer: Comfy Cloud runs on paid tiered plans with seats, and the Kling API nodes bill per generation on top of that based on the duration and mode you choose. You only pay for the clips you actually run, so a short standard-mode test costs less than a long professional-mode render.
